summary:
The arrangement starts at the outer skin level moving deeper into the strata; first is the protective barrier made up primarily of dead cell remnants called corneocytes followed next by pigment-producing melanocytes then immune-responsive langerhans cells before reaching sensory-related merkel cells near underlying tissues.
The sequence begins externally with a tough exterior composed predominantly of flattened, desquamated keratinized epithelial cells known as corneocytes which serve crucially for protection against environmental factors like UV radiation or mechanical damage. Beneath them lie other specialized types including pigmented melanin-secreting melanocytes responsible for tanning responses among others along with immunologically active dendritic-like langerhans cells that play roles within innate immunity systems protecting us further internally while finally nearing inner regions reside tactile receptor-containing merkel cells integral towards somatosensory perception capabilities amongst many physiological functions they perform collectively across these various levels constituting our complex yet vital integumentary system structure. Summary:

Starting superficially, the epidermis consists sequentially of corneocytes (surface), followed by melanocytes containing pigment granules, then langerhans cells involved in immune response, concluding nearer the base with Merkel cells sensing touch stimuli.
Beginning at the most external part, the uppermost layer comprises flat, cornified keratinocyte-derived structures termed 'corneocytes', providing physical defense mechanisms such as resisting ultraviolet light exposure and abrasions etc., beneath lies densely packed melanocytic units producing eumelanins contributing coloration alongside additional functional attributes whereas embedded amidst those two primary groups one finds scattered populations of antigen-presenting phagocytophilic langerhanian entities participating significantly toward non-specific host defenses ultimately tapering off proximally where fine mechanoreceptors represented via highly branched terminations characteristic only to Merkel type II neurons facilitate detection of pressure gradients thus enabling Beneath them lie other specialized types including pigmented melanin-secreting melanocytes responsible for tanning responses among others along with immunologically active dendritic-like langerhans cells that play roles within innate immunity systems protecting us further internally while finally nearing inner regions reside tactile receptor-containing merkel cells integral towards somatosensory perception capabilities amongst many physiological functions they perform collectively across these various levels constituting our complex yet vital integumentary system structure.
